label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. Jane was born on the last day of February in 2001. So, first, I need to figure out what that date is. February usually has 28 days, but in a leap year, it has 29. Is 2001 a leap year? Well, leap years are divisible by 4, but if they're divisible by 100, they must also be divisible by 400. 2001 divided by 4 is 500.25, so not a whole number. So February 2001 had 28 days. Therefore, Jane's birthday is February 28, 2001. Today is her 16th birthday. That means today's date is February 28, 2017? Wait, because 2001 plus 16 years is 2017. But wait, wait. Let me check. Let me count. From 2001 to 2017 is 16 years. So yes, if she was born in 2001, then 2017 would be her 16th birthday. But wait, the problem says "Today is her 16-year-old birthday." So today is February 28, 2017. But the question is asking for the date one year ago from today. So one year before February 28, 2017 would be February 28, 2016. But wait, what if there's a leap year involved here? Well, 2016 is a leap year because 2016 divided by 4 is 504 with no remainder. So February 2016 had 29 days. But the question is about the date one year ago from today. If today is February 28, 2017, then subtracting one year would take us to February 28, 2016. Because even though 2016 is a leap year, the date is still February 28, which exists in both 2016 and 2017. So the answer would be 02/28/2016. Let me check the options. Option C is 02/28/2016. Wait, but wait. Let me make sure there's no trick here. Sometimes when dealing with dates around leap years, if you subtract a year from February 29, you might end up with February 28 or March 1, depending on the convention.
